blurb: A handsome Front Range foothills native of rocky canyons — clusters of waxy white (sometimes pink) star flowers in early summer over richly veined leaves that turn rose-red in fall.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
seasons: Waxy white-to-pink flower clusters early summer; deeply veined leaves turning rose-red in fall; shreddy bark in winter

care.hardiness: A Front Range foothills native, very cold-hardy (USDA zones 3–8, to ~10,000 ft elevation), so Boulder's zone 5b–6a is well within its range.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall into rocky, sharply drained soil, ideally where it gets afternoon shade. Container plants dry quickly and are less root-hardy, so water them and shelter over winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Part to dappled shade is best in hot, lower-elevation Front Range gardens (it grows wild in shaded canyons); it takes more sun at higher, cooler elevations.[1][2]
care.soil: Wants rocky, gritty, well-drained soil and tolerates the alkaline clay of the Front Range; the key is excellent drainage.[1][2]
care.water: Moderate to dry once established — a good xeric choice that needs only occasional water in our climate.[1][2]
care.spacing: A rounded shrub 3–6 ft tall and 4–6 ft wide; space about 4–5 ft apart.[1][2]
care.propagation: Propagates from softwood cuttings and from seed; it can be slow to establish but is long-lived once settled.[1]
care.bloom: Clusters of small, waxy, intensely fragrant white flowers open in May and June; the heart-shaped leaves turn brilliant orange and pink in fall, and flaking reddish-brown bark gives winter interest.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Low maintenance; prune lightly after flowering only to shape, and otherwise leave it alone — it needs little intervention.[1]
care.troubles: Few pests or diseases in well-drained sites; the main risk is rot in soggy or poorly drained ground.[1][2]

references.name: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Jamesia americana

edible.summary: An ornamental native shrub grown for its fragrant flowers and fall color, with no documented edible use.[1]
edible.caution: Waxflower has no known culinary use and isn't eaten; it's valued for its scented blooms, fall foliage, and pollinator value. No part is notably poisonous, but none should be eaten.[1]
